patches.find: This test is addressed in detail in Section 4.2. The dome registers a magnetic anomaly prediction for the August 12, 2026 eclipse: −17 to −21 nT at Ebro
patches.replace:
Credit where due. The August 12, 2026 eclipse prediction is the most methodologically careful element in the entire dome registry: nine named INTERMAGNET stations, per-station magnitudes and error bars, two separate baselines, and git-committed pre-registration before the event. Among kill-shot candidates, this one at least tries to state a prior commitment on observable data. The flaw is not lack of care; it is what the formula is actually doing underneath.

The prior geometric kill-shot. Before the magnetic dip becomes testable, the eclipse path itself is already borrowed from the globe. The August 2026 track — Greenland, Iceland, northern Spain, then the Mediterranean — is computed from Besselian elements fed by JPL's DE440/441 ephemeris, which solves the Sun–Moon–Earth problem under heliocentric Keplerian orbits with a Sun at roughly 150 million km and a Moon at roughly 384,000 km. The dome has a local sun at 5,733 km above a flat 20,015-km-radius disc and a local moon at 2,534 km. On that geometry the shadow cast by the near moon on the flat disc is an ellipse swept by local projective geometry, not the great-circle arc that matches the observed eclipse track. The dome registry supplies no independently derived Besselian elements of its own; it takes the globe-computed path and then runs its magnetic formula against the station-by-station coverage values that path produces. The eclipse geometry is granted by the globe before the magnetic "prediction" begins.

The magnetic prediction is non-discriminating by construction. The dome formula ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF multiplies a 2017 Boulder Observatory measurement (a real globe-era INTERMAGNET baseline) by the globe-computed per-station coverage (0.40–0.95) and an unpublished "Field Strength Factor" (0.642–2.075). Plugged in, the predicted dips at Ebro are reported in the dome's own documents as three inconsistent values — −17 to −21, −28.9, and −29.1 nT — which already undermines the claimed precision. But even the central figure falls inside the 5–25 nT range that Chapman's 1933 ionospheric mechanism predicts under identical eclipse conditions from standard globe physics, with no dome geometry involved. A prediction engineered from globe-era baselines, globe-computed coverage, and a free-parameter multiplier cannot distinguish the dome from the globe — it lands inside Chapman by construction, regardless of how the event turns out. See Section 4.2 for the per-station numbers, the BOU/PIN dual-baseline analysis, and the four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D).

STUB-REPLACED-BY-EXP-159
patches.rationale: Replace Test 5 single-paragraph stub with three-paragraph self-contained kill-shot argument (ISS-941)
